body.text: Every interactive program follows the same pattern: something happens, a variable updates, the page reflects that update. These are three separate steps — the third never happens automatically.

body.desc: User clicks, types, or interacts — something happens
JavaScript updates the value stored in a variable
JavaScript explicitly writes the new value onto the page

lines: // Changing a variable does NOT change the page
// You must always write the new value back explicitly

body.label: Most common beginner mistake
body.text: Students update the variable and assume the page will change automatically. It will not. You must always update the DOM separately.

exercises.title: Find the missing step
tasks: The code below increments count on every log call — but it never prints the updated value correctly.
Read it carefully. Identify which of the three steps is broken or missing.
Fix it so that each console.log prints the correct current value of count.
Then add a fourth variable called doubled and log count multiplied by 2 each time.
exercises.hint: Look at what is being passed to console.log. Is it the variable, or something else?

exercises.title: Build the loop from scratch
tasks: In a blank editor, write all three steps yourself with no reference code.
Declare a count variable. Add a click listener to the document.
Inside the listener: update count, then log 'Count: ' + count.
Run it and click 5 times. Confirm the count increases correctly.
Challenge: log a different message when count reaches exactly 5.
exercises.hint: Step 2 is count = count + 1. Step 3 is console.log(...). Both go inside the listener.
